edges.evidence_quote: Los Machos was a Colombian drug trafficking paramilitary organization . The group started as Montoya's security force.

edges.evidence_quote: Diego León Montoya con sus aliados y su ejército personal que denominó como "Los Machos"

descriptions: Insane Latin Brothers are a member of the People Nation.
edges.evidence_quote: Gangs that are members of the People Nation alliance regardless of status of activity include the following: - Insane Latin Brothers

descriptions: Infobox lists OC Nasty Squad as an ally of PDC.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: PIF (Paid In Full), CFR (Corleone Family Riderz), OC (Organised Crime) Nasty Squad, 031 (0-Tray 1 Bloods)

Popular Front for the Liberation of Pale.description: Palestinian Marxist-Leninist organization founded in 1967 by George Habash. It is the second-largest faction in the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) and opposes recognition of Israel, advocating a one-state solution. The group pioneered aircraft hijackings in the late 1960s-70s and its military wing is the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades. Designated a terrorist organization by the US, Japan, Canada, and the EU.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: The gang funded the PFLP through robberies.
edges.evidence_quote: Between December 1972 and May 1989, they committed robberies and sent the money to the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: KAK leaders visited PFLP in Jordan.
edges.evidence_quote: Appel and Ulla Hauton visited the PFLP in Jordan from September 1 to 22, 1970.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: KUF members trained in PFLP camps.
edges.evidence_quote: In October 1969, two KUF members were the first Westerners to train in PFLP training camps in Jordan.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: KA maintained contact with PFLP through a designated intelligence chief.
edges.evidence_quote: PFLP Intelligence chief Marwan El-Fahoum had been assigned as KA's new primary PFLP contact in May 1979.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: The gang financed the PFLP through robbery proceeds.
edges.evidence_quote: Between December 1972 and May 1989, they committed robberies and sent the money to the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP is listed as an ally of the PKK in the infobox.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: ... Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: ... Non-state allies: ... Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP is listed as an ally of PIJ in the infobox.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: ... PFLP

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades is the paramilitary wing of PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: The military wing of the PFLP is called the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades is the paramilitary wing of the PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: The military wing of the PFLP is called the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP was a founding member of APF.
edges.evidence_quote: In December 1993 PFLP withdrew from the PLO and became one of the ten founding members of the Damascus-based Alliance of Palestinian Forces.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP originated from the ANM.
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP grew out of the Harakat al-Qawmiyyin al-Arab, or Arab Nationalist Movement (ANM), founded in 1953 by George Habash.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: DFLP split from PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: In 1969, the Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine (DFLP) formed as a separate, ostensibly Maoist, organization under Nayef Hawatmeh and Yasser Abd Rabbo, initially as the PDFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP opposes Fatah's moderate stance.
edges.evidence_quote: A secular organization, the PFLP has generally taken a hard line on Palestinian national aspirations, opposing the more moderate stance of Fatah.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP and Fatah are both members of the PLO, with Fatah being the largest.
edges.evidence_quote: It has consistently been the second-largest of the groups forming the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO), the largest being Fatah.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP fights alongside Hamas in the Gaza war.
edges.evidence_quote: More recently, the group has participated in the ongoing Gaza war alongside Hamas and other allied Palestinian factions.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP's military wing fought alongside Hamas during the Gaza war.
edges.evidence_quote: Gaza war (2023–present): the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades published videos of it storming Israeli watchtowers during the October 7 attacks, and has since fought alongside Hamas and other allied factions in multiple battles inside the Gaza Strip.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP developed contacts with Hezbollah.
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP developed contacts at this time with Islamic fundamentalist groups linked to Iran – both Palestinian Hamas, and the Lebanon-based Hezbollah.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP developed ties with Japanese Red Army.
edges.evidence_quote: From its foundation, the PFLP sought superpower patrons, early on developing ties with the Soviet Union, the People's Republic of China, and, at various times, with regional powers such as Syria, South Yemen, Libya, North Korea, and Iraq, as well as with left-wing groups around the world, including the FARC and the Japanese Red Army.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Japanese Red Army collaborated with a PFLP faction in the Lod Airport massacre.
edges.evidence_quote: On 30 May 1972, 28 passengers were gunned down at Ben Gurion International Airport by members of the Japanese Red Army in collaboration with the PFLP's Waddie Haddad in what became known as the Lod Airport massacre.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP's Jordan branch became the Jordanian Popular Democratic Unity Party.
edges.evidence_quote: In 1990, the PFLP transformed its Jordan branch into a separate political party, the Jordanian Popular Democratic Unity Party.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP is a member group of the PLO.
edges.evidence_quote: It has consistently been the second-largest of the groups forming the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO), the largest being Fatah.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Palestinian Liberation Front merged into PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: Included within the discussions, as well as the ANM (including the RYO), were Heroes of Return (officially a collaboration with the PLO but actually under ANM control), Ahmed Jibril's Syria-based Palestinian Liberation Front, and a group of Jordanian Nasserists led by the former army officer Ahmed Za'rur. These groups combined to form the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine (PFLP).

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP allied with Palestinian People's Party.
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP has therefore formed alliances with other leftist groups formed within the Palestinian Authority, including the Palestinian People's Party and the Popular Resistance Committees of Gaza.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PPSF split from PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: In 1967, Palestinian Popular Struggle Front (PPSF) broke away from the PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command
descriptions: PFLP-GC split from PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: In October 1968, Ahmed Jibril led a break away from the PFLP to form the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command (PFLP-GC).

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP allied with Popular Resistance Committees.
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP has therefore formed alliances with other leftist groups formed within the Palestinian Authority, including the Palestinian People's Party and the Popular Resistance Committees of Gaza.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Popular Revolutionary Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PRFLP split from PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: In 1972, the Popular Revolutionary Front for the Liberation of Palestine was formed following a split in PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP developed ties with FARC.
edges.evidence_quote: From its foundation, the PFLP sought superpower patrons, early on developing ties with the Soviet Union, the People's Republic of China, and, at various times, with regional powers such as Syria, South Yemen, Libya, North Korea, and Iraq, as well as with left-wing groups around the world, including the FARC and the Japanese Red Army.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP members were killed in a clash with the SLA.
edges.evidence_quote: On 8 June 1990 a clash occurred near Shuwayya in South Lebanon in which four members of the PFLP were killed by the South Lebanon Army (SLA).

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Stasi trained PFLP members.
edges.evidence_quote: Similar treatment was meted out to Palestinian terrorists from the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine

Chicanos Motorcycle Club.description: International outlaw motorcycle club founded in Pritzwalk, Germany on 23 August 2003. Acts as a support club for the Bandidos, with chapters in multiple countries including Germany, Netherlands, Norway, Russia, Serbia, Sweden, Ukraine, and UAE. Known for violent conflicts with Hells Angels and other groups. Colors are red and gold, insignia features two crossed sabres.

descriptions: Infobox lists Chicanos MC as ally.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Brother Speed MC , Chicanos MC , Diablos MC , Mobshitters MC , Outlaws MC , Rock Machine MC , Satudarah MC , X-Team , Los Zetas

descriptions: Chicanos support Bandidos in Germany.
edges.evidence_quote: The German Bandidos are supported by the Chicanos, Diablos and X-Team.

descriptions: Bandidos are affiliated with Chicanos in Norway.
edges.evidence_quote: The Norwegian Bandidos are affiliated with the Chicanos and X-Team

descriptions: Chicanos are affiliates of Bandidos in Sweden.
edges.evidence_quote: Affiliates of the Bandidos in Sweden include the Chicanos

descriptions: Chicanos are a support group for Bandidos.
edges.evidence_quote: The perpetrators were members of the Chicanos, a support group of the Bandidos motorcycle gang.

descriptions: Black Cobra and Chicanos are rivals per infobox.
edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: AK81 , X-Team , Bandidos , Chicanos , Hells Angels

descriptions: Chicanos act as a support club for Bandidos.
edges.evidence_quote: The Chicanos are a support club (called puppet clubs by law enforcement ) for the Bandidos.

descriptions: Chicanos and Black Cobra had a conflict in Sweden.
edges.evidence_quote: The Chicanos in Dalsland became involved in a conflict with the Black Cobra gang after attempting to recruit Black Cobra members.

descriptions: Brigade 81, affiliated with Hells Angels, attacked Chicanos clubhouse.
edges.evidence_quote: On 5 June 2009, members of the Hells Angels-affiliated Brigade 81 raided the Chicanos' clubhouse in Ludwigsfelde , leaving several club members injured.

descriptions: District 61 member shot at Chicanos members.
edges.evidence_quote: In June 2013, a member of District 61 – a group comprised partially of former Underdogs members – shot in self-defense at two Chicanos members in Halle city center, although nobody was wounded.

descriptions: Chicanos and Hells Angels engaged in violent clashes.
edges.evidence_quote: Chicanos members assaulted the president of the Hells Angels ' nomads chapter in a nightclub in Eberswalde on 6 December 2008.

descriptions: A No Surrender chapter patched over to become a Chicanos chapter.
edges.evidence_quote: Sweden's first Chicanos branch opened in Vänersborg in 2012, with a patch-over of a No Surrender chapter.

descriptions: Chicanos and Underdogs engaged in multiple violent confrontations.
edges.evidence_quote: Members of the Chicanos and the Underdogs Motorcycle Club fought in the street in Halle city center on 26 October 2010.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Chicanos MC

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Road Runners MC

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Road Runners MC , Bandidos , Mongols MC , Annihilators MC , Dirty Dozen MC , Vigilantes MC Eleventh Hour RC

Dixon Bloods.description: Street gang based in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, founded in 2006. Involved in drug and weapons trafficking, operating from a highrise complex on Dixon Road. Dismantled by police in 2013 through Project Traveller, resulting in 43 arrests and seizure of 40 guns. Still considered active as of the article's information.

descriptions: A founding member of Dixon Bloods was allegedly a former member of G-Shine Bloods, indicating a structural link.
edges.evidence_quote: Unconfirmed rumours state that one of the founding members of the gang was a former member of a New York-based gang known as G-Shine Bloods, who had relocated to the neighborhood in the early 2000s.

descriptions: Dixon City Bloods listed as rival in infobox.
edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Bahala Na Gang , Commisso 'ndrina , Dhak crime group , Dixon City Bloods , Duhre crime group , FOB Killers gang(FK gang) , Game Tight Soldiers gang , Kang Crime Group(BIBO gang) , Luppino crime family , Red Scorpions (2017–present) , Renegades Motorcycle Club(Canada) , Bloods -affiliated Somalian street gangs , Triads , United Nations (gang) , White Boy Posse , 856 gang

descriptions: Listed as a rival in the infobox.
edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Dixon City Bloods

Maniac Latin Disciples.description: Predominantly Hispanic street gang based in Chicago, Illinois, founded in 1966 as a baseball club that evolved into a full-fledged gang. They are part of the Folks Alliance and are involved in drug trafficking, assault, auto theft, robbery, homicide, money laundering, and weapons trafficking. The gang has an estimated 7,500-10,000 members and operates in multiple states across the U.S.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Folk Nation , Insane Deuces, Simon City Royals , Insane Spanish Cobras , Maniac Latin Disciples , C-Notes, Insane North Side Popes

edges.evidence_quote: From then on the Latin Disciples Nation went to war with the Latin Kings.

descriptions: Latin Scorpions merged with another club to form the Latin Disciples in 1966.
edges.evidence_quote: The Latin Scorpions merged with another Latino club ran by Ernesto “Capper” Flores and turned into a full-fledged gang in 1966, calling themselves the Latin Disciples.

edges.evidence_quote: The Hells Angels Vikings were 'in a constant state of war' with the official Hells Angels chapter in London and were renamed the Vikings in 1975.

Caltagirone Mafia family.description: The Caltagirone Mafia family is a Cosa Nostra crime family based in Caltagirone, Sicily, founded in 1982 by Francesco 'Ciccio' La Rocca. It is involved in extortion, drug trafficking, and political manipulation. The family rose to prominence through alliances with the Catania Mafia and Corleonesi. Despite La Rocca's imprisonment and death in 2020, the family remains active.

descriptions: Caltagirone Mafia allied with Catania Mafia.
edges.evidence_quote: His alliances with powerful Cosa Nostra factions, particularly the Catania Mafia and the Corleonesi led by Totò Riina, further elevated the status of the Caltagirone Mafia clan within the Sicilian Mafia hierarchy.

descriptions: Caltagirone Mafia was loyal to Santapaola Mafia clan.
edges.evidence_quote: Despite longstanding loyalty to the Santapaola Mafia clan, a brief moment of internal dissent occurred during Ciccio’s imprisonment in the late 1990s.

descriptions: Caltagirone Mafia allied with Corleonesi.
edges.evidence_quote: His alliances with powerful Cosa Nostra factions, particularly the Catania Mafia and the Corleonesi led by Totò Riina, further elevated the status of the Caltagirone Mafia clan within the Sicilian Mafia hierarchy.

descriptions: A faction of Caltagirone Mafia supported the Mazzei clan in a coup attempt against Santapaola.
edges.evidence_quote: His nephew, Gesualdo La Rocca, supported an attempted coup by the rival Mazzei clan to displace the Santapaola leadership in Catania.

descriptions: Caltagirone Mafia maintains ties with Nardo clan.
edges.evidence_quote: The clan maintains ties with other criminal groups, including the Santapaola-Ercolano of Catania and the Nardo clan of Lentini.

38th Street Gang.description: The 38th Street Gang is an American criminal street gang in Los Angeles, California, composed mainly of Mexican-Americans. Founded in the 1920s, it is one of the oldest street gangs in Los Angeles and is known for its involvement in the Sleepy Lagoon murder trial and Zoot Suit Riots. The gang engages in drug trafficking, assault, robbery, and other criminal activities, and is controlled by the Mexican Mafia. It remains active as of the present.

Velentzas Organization.description: Greek-American criminal organization operating in New York City, founded by Peter Kourakos. Under Spyredon Velentzas, it controlled illegal gambling in Astoria, Queens, and had ties to the Lucchese crime family. Declined after Velentzas' 1992 murder conviction and life sentence. Still active as of 2024.

descriptions: The Gambino family opposed Velentzas Organization's expansion into their gambling territory, leading to threats and conflict.
edges.evidence_quote: The FBI caught on wiretaps Gambino family boss John Gotti threatening to have Spiro killed for moving into the gambling territory controlled by the Gambino family.

descriptions: Bonde dos 40 has been violently battling PCM for control.
edges.evidence_quote: the group became known for instigating riots in prisons and in the Pedrinhas Penitentiary Complex against the Primeiro Comando do Maranhão (PCM), and for carrying out attacks on the streets, resulting in the deaths of over 100 inmates. Since then, PCM and B40 have been violently battling for control over stilt neighborhoods and peripheral communities, causing dozens of deaths and injuries.

descriptions: B40 and PCM fought violently for control of neighborhoods.
edges.evidence_quote: Desde então, o PCM e B40 passaram a lutar violentamente pelos domínios

Galatolo Mafia clan.description: The Galatolo Mafia clan was a criminal family from the Acquasanta neighborhood of Palermo, active from the 1950s until 2014. They controlled the Palermo docks and were involved in a violent conflict over the fruit and vegetable wholesale market in the 1950s. The clan was devastated by internal betrayal and later re-emerged, aligning with the Corleonesi in the 1980s.

descriptions: Galatolo clan fought Bontade clan over control of wholesale market racket.
edges.evidence_quote: The Acquasanta Mafia clan tried to muscle in on the protection racket that traditionally belonged the 'Mafia of the Gardens' — such as the Greco, Bontade and Sorci clans from the southeast of Palermo

descriptions: Cavataio faction betrayed and killed Galatolo leaders to take over the clan.
edges.evidence_quote: An up and coming member of the Acquasanta family, Michele Cavataio, together with other mafiosi, took advantage of the situation to eliminate the top ranks of the Galatolo clan and assume control for himself.

descriptions: Galatolo clan was subordinate to and allied with Madonia clan.
edges.evidence_quote: The Acquasanta family thus now answered to the Madonia clan, strong allies of the Galatolos.

descriptions: Galatolo clan ruled the Resuttana mandamento.
edges.evidence_quote: even coming to rule the entire Resuttana mandamento in the early 21st century.

descriptions: Galatolo clan allied with Corleonesi faction in the 1970s.
edges.evidence_quote: Both Galatolo and Madonia soon became close to the up and rising faction of the Corleonesi, ruled by Salvatore Riina

descriptions: Galatolo clan fought Greco clan over control of wholesale market racket.
edges.evidence_quote: The Acquasanta Mafia clan tried to muscle in on the protection racket that traditionally belonged the 'Mafia of the Gardens' — such as the Greco, Bontade and Sorci clans from the southeast of Palermo

descriptions: Galatolo clan cooperated with Corleonesi and their allies including Michele Greco's faction.
edges.evidence_quote: During the Second Mafia War, the Galatolo family of Vincenzo and his brothers Giuseppe and Raffaele played a crucial role in many of the murders orchestrated by the Corleonesi.

descriptions: Galatolo clan was under the authority of Partanna-Mondello mandamento.
edges.evidence_quote: their territory was initially under the jurisdiction of the Partanna-Mondello mandamento ruled by Rosario Riccobono

descriptions: Galatolo clan fought Sorci clan over control of wholesale market racket.
edges.evidence_quote: The Acquasanta Mafia clan tried to muscle in on the protection racket that traditionally belonged the 'Mafia of the Gardens' — such as the Greco, Bontade and Sorci clans from the southeast of Palermo

descriptions: The Grecos and Acquasanta clan were rivals in the Palermo wholesale market war in the mid-1950s.
edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Acquasanta Mafia clan (Palermo wholesale market war in the mid 1950s)

descriptions: The Greco clan rivaled the Acquasanta Mafia clan over the Palermo wholesale market.
edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Acquasanta Mafia clan (Palermo wholesale market war in the mid 1950s)

descriptions: Tribesmen became a prospective Hells Angels chapter in 1983.
edges.evidence_quote: the Tribesmen became a prospective Hells Angels chapter

descriptions: A Tribesmen member shot the Killer Beez leader, indicating conflict.
edges.evidence_quote: On 26 April 2019 Killer Beez head Josh Masters, a prominent gang member was shot by a member of the Tribesmen at a Harley Davidson dealership in Mount Wellington, Auckland.

descriptions: Ongoing conflicts between the two gangs led to multiple shootings.
edges.evidence_quote: In May 2022, conflicts between the Tribesmen and Killer Beez led to shootings in South Auckland.

descriptions: Killer Beez was founded as a spin-off of the Tribesmen gang.
edges.evidence_quote: They were founded in 2003, as a large rogue street gang in Ōtara, as a spin-off of the larger 'Tribesmen' gang.
